past_medical_history: Squamous cell carcinoma of the left lung, stage IIIB, diagnosed 14 months ago; completed first-line carboplatin/gemcitabine with partial response, then progressed; currently receiving nivolumab (anti-PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor) as second-line palliative therapy for past 7 months with confirmed tumor regression
Left temporal epidermoid cyst (intracranial), stable on serial brain MRI for over 10 years, incidental finding
Remote focal epilepsy with right-hand tonic convulsions secondary to epidermoid cyst, seizure-free for 3.5 years on lamotrigine
Hypertension, well controlled on bisoprolol and lisinopril
Chronic kidney disease stage 3 (baseline creatinine 1.2-1.4 mg/dL)
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, GOLD stage II, on inhaled bronchodilators
Chronic low back pain managed with baclofen

medications.indication: Squamous cell lung carcinoma, second-line palliative therapy
Chronic low back pain with muscle spasm

family_history: Father: died of myocardial infarction at age 68
Mother: died of pancreatic cancer at age 74
No family history of autoimmune disorders, epilepsy, or dementia

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 40 pack-year history, quit 3 years ago at cancer diagnosis
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in ground-floor apartment
social_history.exercise: Limited by COPD, short walks around the block

neurological_exam.mental_status: Glasgow Coma Scale 7 (E2V1M4): eye opening to pain only, no verbal response (intubation not present, patient simply mute), withdrawal to painful stimuli. Not oriented. Unable to follow commands. No spontaneous speech. Intermittent periods of agitation with purposeless limb movements alternating with unresponsiveness.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive to light. Corneal reflexes present bilaterally. Preexisting left lower motor neuron facial paresis (documented in prior notes, related to epidermoid cyst). Oculocephalic reflexes intact. No gaze deviation. Gag reflex present.
neurological_exam.motor: Withdrawal to pain in all four extremities. No focal weakness appreciable in this obtunded state. Recurrent multifocal myoclonus observed in bilateral upper and lower extremities, arrhythmic, stimulus-sensitive. Tone mildly increased in bilateral lower extremities.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdrawal to noxious stimuli symmetric in all four extremities. Detailed sensory examination not possible due to altered mental status.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 3+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Left Babinski sign present (upgoing plantar response on left). Right plantar response equivocal.
neurological_exam.coordination: Not assessable due to altered mental status and myoclonus.
neurological_exam.additional: Recurrent generalized myoclonic jerks involving face, trunk, and all extremities, occurring every 10-30 seconds, exacerbated by tactile stimulation. No meningismus. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. No orofacial dyskinesias noted. Intermittent autonomic instability with heart rate fluctuations between 55 and 110 bpm and diaphoresis.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive unresponsiveness, apathy, and loss of speech over 24 hours with recurrent myoclonic jerks
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Dupont is a 76-year-old right-handed retired electrician with a complex medical history including stage IIIB squamous cell lung carcinoma currently on nivolumab immunotherapy, a known stable left temporal epidermoid cyst with remote seizure history, hypertension, CKD stage 3, and COPD, who presents to the emergency department with progressive neurological deterioration over the preceding 24 hours. According to his wife, the patient was at his baseline functional state until the day before presentation, when she noticed he became unusually quiet and withdrawn during breakfast. Over the course of the morning, he became increasingly apathetic, sitting motionlessly and staring blankly. By the afternoon, his speech had deteriorated from sparse, single-word responses to complete mutism. He was unable to follow simple commands by evening and did not recognize family members who visited. His wife called emergency services when she witnessed repetitive jerking movements of his arms and legs that would recur every few minutes, each episode lasting 5-10 seconds. She reports no preceding headache, no fever or chills, no cough, no vomiting, no falls or head trauma, and no recent medication changes. His most recent nivolumab infusion was 12 days ago, which was his 14th cycle. He has been seizure-free for 3.5 years on stable-dose lamotrigine, and the last oncology visit 3 weeks ago confirmed continued tumor regression on imaging. His baclofen and clorazepate doses have been unchanged for months. The wife specifically denies any recent infections, travel, tick bites, or new medications. She reports he had been mildly more fatigued over the preceding 2 weeks but attributed this to his cancer treatment. The paramedics documented a Glasgow Coma Scale of 8 in the field with ongoing myoclonic jerks. On arrival to the emergency department, initial CT head showed no acute hemorrhage and the known left temporal epidermoid cyst appeared stable. The clinical team's initial concern was for ongoing seizure activity or prolonged postictal state given his epilepsy history, and he was empirically loaded with levetiracetam intravenously.

background.reactivity: Minimal reactivity to auditory and noxious stimulation

findings.morphology: Intermittent rhythmic delta activity, moderate amplitude
findings.state: Present during entire recording, more prominent than generalized slowing

findings.morphology: Sharp transients with aftergoing slow waves, moderate amplitude
findings.state: Intermittent, not evolving into electrographic seizure patterns

artifacts.description: Intermittent high-frequency artifact from myoclonic jerks, partially obscuring underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Not performed due to clinical condition
activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to clinical condition

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG showing moderate diffuse encephalopathy with superimposed left temporal focal slowing and sporadic epileptiform discharges. Intermittent generalized periodic discharges with triphasic morphology noted. No definite electrographic seizures captured during this recording. The left temporal focal changes likely relate at least in part to the known epidermoid cyst. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording duration 45 minutes. Significant muscle artifact during myoclonic episodes. No prior EEG available for comparison.

findings.location: Left temporal lobe, extra-axial, centered in the middle cranial fossa extending into the cerebellopontine angle cistern

signal_characteristics.DWI: Restricted diffusion (bright on DWI, dark on ADC), characteristic of epidermoid

findings.mass_effect: Mild compression of the left temporal lobe without significant midline shift

findings.location: Periventricular and deep white matter, bilateral, Fazekas grade 2

additional_observations: No leptomeningeal enhancement on post-gadolinium sequences
No new parenchymal enhancing lesions to suggest intracranial metastatic disease
Ventricles symmetric and appropriate for age
Stable appearance of left temporal epidermoid compared to prior MRI 4 months ago
mri.impression: Stable left temporal epidermoid cyst without interval change. Age-appropriate cerebral atrophy and chronic microvascular ischemic disease. No evidence of intracranial metastases, leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, acute stroke, or radiographic encephalitis. Normal-appearing medial temporal lobes bilaterally. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in leads V4-V6
Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon index 38 mm)
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 92 bpm with nonspecific ST-T changes and left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. No acute ischemic changes.

Glucose: 38 mg/dL (low; normal: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.35 (serum glucose 108 mg/dL; CSF/serum ratio borderline low, normal >0.6)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen. HSV_PCR: Negative. VZV_PCR: Negative. Enterovirus_PCR: Negative. CSF_lactate: 4.1 mmol/L (elevated; normal: 1.1-2.4 mmol/L). Cytology: No malignant cells identified. Reactive lymphocytes present.. Aspiration-type consolidation in right lower lobe — new, correlate clinically for aspiration pneumonia

New right lower lobe consolidation suspicious for aspiration pneumonia in the setting of decreased level of consciousness. No evidence of new metastatic disease. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: checkpoint inhibitor nivolumab autoimmune encephalitis anti-NMDAR

results.title: Neurological immune-related adverse events of checkpoint inhibitors: a systematic review
results.key_finding: Neurological irAEs occur in 1-5% of patients on checkpoint inhibitors. Encephalitis is the most common serious neurological irAE. Anti-NMDAR encephalitis has been reported in association with anti-PD-1 therapy, though classical paraneoplastic antibodies are more frequently negative.

results.title: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis secondary to nivolumab: a case report and literature review
results.key_finding: Checkpoint inhibitor-associated anti-NMDAR encephalitis is a rare but recognized immune-related adverse event. Onset typically occurs 2-6 months after initiation. Treatment includes checkpoint inhibitor discontinuation, high-dose corticosteroids, and escalation to IVIG or plasmapheresis if needed. Prognosis is variable but early recognition improves outcomes.

results.title: Management of immune checkpoint inhibitor-related neurological adverse events: a consensus guideline
results.key_finding: Grade 3-4 neurological irAEs warrant permanent discontinuation of checkpoint inhibitor therapy. First-line treatment is high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1g daily for 3-5 days). IVIG or plasmapheresis should be added if no improvement within 48-72 hours. Anti-NMDAR antibody testing should be included in the workup of any encephalitis occurring during checkpoint inhibitor therapy.

output.summary: Checkpoint inhibitor-associated autoimmune encephalitis, including anti-NMDAR encephalitis, is an uncommon but well-documented immune-related adverse event (irAE) of anti-PD-1 therapy such as nivolumab. It typically presents weeks to months after therapy initiation with subacute neuropsychiatric symptoms, seizures, and encephalopathy. Diagnosis requires a high index of suspicion, as standard paraneoplastic antibody panels may not include anti-NMDAR testing. Treatment involves permanent discontinuation of the checkpoint inhibitor, high-dose corticosteroids, and consideration of IVIG or plasmapheresis. Early recognition is critical for improved outcomes.

output.proposed: High-dose IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for patient on nivolumab, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, bisoprolol, lisinopril
interactions: Methylprednisolone + nivolumab: Corticosteroids may reduce the efficacy of checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy by suppressing the immune response. However, in the setting of severe irAEs (grade 3-4 encephalitis), corticosteroids are indicated and checkpoint inhibitor should be permanently discontinued.
Methylprednisolone + lamotrigine: Corticosteroids can induce hepatic glucuronidation enzymes, potentially reducing lamotrigine levels by 20-30%. Monitor lamotrigine levels and adjust dose if needed.
Methylprednisolone may cause hyperglycemia, fluid retention, and hypertension — monitor closely given existing CKD and hypertension.
warnings: High-dose corticosteroids in an immunocompromised oncology patient increase infection risk — aspiration pneumonia already suspected
Consider PPI for GI prophylaxis during high-dose steroid therapy
output.formulary_status: Formulary — approved for immune-related adverse events
alternatives: IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days — if steroid-refractory or contraindicated
Plasmapheresis — for severe or refractory cases
Rituximab — for second-line immunotherapy in refractory anti-NMDAR encephalitis

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis secondary to nivolumab (checkpoint inhibitor-associated immune-related adverse event)

differential.diagnosis: Epileptic seizure or prolonged postictal state
differential.key_distinguishing: Known epilepsy history with left temporal epidermoid cyst. However, therapeutic lamotrigine level, EEG showing no continuous seizure activity on initial study, and lack of improvement with levetiracetam and midazolam argue against this as sole explanation.
CSF HSV and VZV PCR negative. No temporal lobe signal on MRI. Afebrile presentation. However, lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein are consistent, requiring exclusion.
Known lung carcinoma raises concern. However, MRI shows no leptomeningeal enhancement, CSF cytology negative for malignant cells, and tumor is responding to therapy.
Sodium 132 and creatinine 1.3 are mildly abnormal but insufficient to cause GCS 7. Ammonia normal. No hepatic dysfunction.

Comprehensive classical paraneoplastic panel (Hu, Ri, Yo, amphiphysin, CV2, Ma2, SOX1, GAD65) is negative. However, anti-NMDAR antibodies are not included in standard panels and must be specifically requested.

CT shows possible aspiration in right lower lobe, and CRP mildly elevated. However, patient is afebrile, WBC normal, and septic encephalopathy would not explain the CSF pleocytosis and elevated protein.

optimal_actions.action: Perform urgent head CT to exclude hemorrhage or mass lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No acute hemorrhage; stable epidermoid cyst

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to evaluate for stroke, metastases, leptomeningeal disease, and encephalitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stable epidermoid cyst; no metastases, leptomeningeal enhancement, stroke, or radiographic encephalitis

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with comprehensive CSF analysis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose, elevated lactate, negative infectious studies

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for seizures and encephalopathy pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse slowing with left temporal focal changes, intermittent periodic discharges

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive metabolic workup including electrolytes, glucose, ammonia, liver function, TSH, and drug levels
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia, mild thrombocytopenia, therapeutic lamotrigine level; no metabolic cause sufficient for this degree of encephalopathy

optimal_actions.action: Request continuous EEG monitoring for 24-48 hours
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Extreme delta brush pattern (pathognomonic for anti-NMDAR encephalitis); subclinical seizures

optimal_actions.action: Order anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing in BOTH serum and CSF
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Serum negative, CSF positive — confirming diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Permanently discontinue nivolumab and initiate high-dose IV methylprednisolone
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Treatment of grade 3-4 neurological irAE per guidelines

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on checkpoint inhibitor-associated encephalitis management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting corticosteroids, IVIG, and checkpoint inhibitor discontinuation

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for immunosuppressive therapy in the context of current medications
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Corticosteroids may reduce lamotrigine levels; infection risk increased

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis — the CSF profile is critical for differentiating autoimmune from infectious encephalitis
Order anti-NMDA receptor antibodies specifically (serum AND CSF) — standard paraneoplastic panels do not include anti-NMDAR testing
Permanently discontinue nivolumab — grade 3-4 neurological irAEs require permanent checkpoint inhibitor discontinuation
Initiate high-dose IV corticosteroids — first-line treatment for severe checkpoint inhibitor-associated autoimmune encephalitis
Obtain continuous EEG monitoring — critical for detecting subclinical seizures and extreme delta brush pattern
Rule out infectious encephalitis (HSV, VZV, bacterial) before or concurrent with immunotherapy
contraindicated_actions: Continuing nivolumab therapy — must be permanently discontinued for grade 3-4 neurological irAEs
Attributing encephalopathy solely to postictal state without further workup — delayed diagnosis worsens prognosis
Administering additional antipsychotics — can worsen movement disorder and autonomic instability in anti-NMDAR encephalitis
Performing brain biopsy — not indicated when antibody testing and CSF analysis can establish diagnosis
Initiating high-dose immunosuppression without excluding active infection — aspiration pneumonia must be treated concurrently
key_reasoning_points: The temporal relationship between nivolumab therapy (28 weeks, 14th cycle) and onset of encephalopathy is a critical clue to checkpoint inhibitor-associated autoimmune encephalitis
Known left temporal epidermoid cyst with seizure history creates a strong red herring — the clinical team's initial assumption of postictal state or breakthrough seizures is reasonable but incomplete
Normal brain MRI does NOT exclude anti-NMDAR encephalitis — approximately 50% of cases have normal MRI
CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and low glucose — the inflammatory profile points away from simple postictal state or metabolic encephalopathy
Standard paraneoplastic antibody panels (Hu, Ri, Yo, amphiphysin, CV2, Ma2, SOX1, GAD65) do NOT include anti-NMDAR testing — it must be specifically requested
Serum anti-NMDAR antibodies can be negative in approximately 14% of cases with positive CSF antibodies — CSF testing is essential
Extreme delta brush on continuous EEG is highly characteristic of anti-NMDAR encephalitis and may be the key diagnostic clue before antibody results return
The mild hyponatremia (132 mmol/L) is an inadequate explanation for GCS 7 and should prompt search for alternative diagnoses
Checkpoint inhibitor-associated encephalitis requires both permanent drug discontinuation AND immunosuppressive treatment
Anti-NMDAR encephalitis in elderly males is atypical — the classical demographic is young women with ovarian teratoma — but immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy has expanded the recognized demographic

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to multiple confounders: known left temporal epidermoid cyst with seizure history creating a strong red herring for postictal state; elderly male patient (atypical demographic for anti-NMDAR encephalitis); normal brain MRI; standard paraneoplastic panel negative (anti-NMDAR not included); mild hyponatremia as alternative explanation; checkpoint inhibitor-associated etiology requiring specific clinical suspicion
